questions.prompt: Cassius's recruitment of Brutus and the assassination plot's growth form this rising-action element.
questions.explanation: In Julius Caesar (1599), Shakespeare builds complications through Acts 1-2: Cassius's flattering speech to Brutus ('Why, man, he doth bestride the narrow world like a Colossus'), the forged letters, the meeting at Pompey's Porch, and Calpurnia's omens. RL.9-10.3 requires students to analyze how complex characters develop, and tracking these escalating events is essential. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice often asks how rising action develops a central idea like ambition or republicanism.

questions.prompt: The assassination of Caesar in Act 3, Scene 1 with 'Et tu, Brute?' functions as this plot turning point.
questions.explanation: Shakespeare positions the assassination at 3.1 as climax: Caesar's 'Et tu, Brute? Then fall, Caesar!' marks the pivot from political plotting to civic chaos. Some critics argue the true climax is Antony's funeral oration later in 3.2 that turns the crowd. RL.9-10.5 covers how an author's structural choices create meaning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on power and betrayal often hinge on this scene. AP Lit later requires deeper structural analysis of the play's dual climax.

questions.prompt: Brutus's idealism foils Cassius's pragmatic envy in Julius Caesar, developing this central conflict.
questions.explanation: Shakespeare pairs Brutus (republican principle, 'not that I loved Caesar less, but that I loved Rome more') with Cassius (personal envy, 'why should that name be sounded more than yours?') to dramatize how mixed motives doom the conspiracy. RL.9-10.3 covers character analysis through interaction. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on conspiracy or political idealism often use this foil. AP Lit demands precise motive analysis.

questions.prompt: Mark Antony's 'Friends, Romans, countrymen' speech sparking civil war is this kind of plot consequence.
questions.explanation: After Caesar's death, the play's falling action accelerates: Antony's funeral oration (3.2) turns Rome against the conspirators, sparking the civil war in Acts 4-5. RL.9-10.5 covers structure analysis. The play's structural symmetry (rise of conspiracy / fall of conspirators) mirrors classical tragedy. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on rhetoric routinely use Antony's speech. AP Lang treats it as a canonical rhetorical analysis text alongside Lincoln and King.

questions.prompt: The ghost of Caesar appearing to Brutus before Philippi serves as this narrative device.
questions.explanation: Caesar's ghost appears to Brutus in 4.3 saying 'thou shalt see me at Philippi,' foreshadowing Brutus's defeat and suicide. Shakespeare layers omens: Calpurnia's dream, the soothsayer's warning, the storm. RL.9-10.5 covers structural choices that create suspense or meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests foreshadowing recognition. AP Lit analysis essays often hinge on foreshadowing's thematic effect. Common Core lists Shakespeare for Grades 9-10.

questions.prompt: The deserted island in this Golding novel allows boys' descent into savagery to unfold.
questions.explanation: William Golding's Lord of the Flies (1954) uses the deserted Pacific island as setting that strips away civilization. The conch shell, the signal fire, the Beast, and Piggy's glasses all function as setting-bound symbols. RL.9-10.3 covers how setting drives plot and character. The setting is essential to Golding's argument that civilization is a thin veneer. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on human nature, savagery, or order use LOTF. AP Lit treats it as a canonical 20th-century text.

questions.prompt: Auschwitz in Elie Wiesel's memoir serves as setting and this kind of historical reality.
questions.explanation: Auschwitz-Birkenau, the Nazi concentration and extermination camp, is the central setting of Elie Wiesel's Night (1958/1960). The setting is historical reality, not metaphor. Wiesel's compressed prose ('Never shall I forget that night, the first night in camp') depends on the setting's specificity. RI.9-10.6 covers memoir and witness; RL.9-10.3 covers character in setting. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on genocide, faith, and survival use Night extensively.

questions.prompt: Stanley Kowalski's New Orleans apartment in Streetcar functions as this kind of setting for class conflict.
questions.explanation: Tennessee Williams's A Streetcar Named Desire (1947) sets the Kowalski apartment in the French Quarter of New Orleans as cramped, hot, working-class space that crushes Blanche DuBois's faded Southern aristocratic identity. The streetcar names ('Desire,' 'Cemeteries') symbolize Blanche's psychological journey. RL.9-10.3 covers setting analysis. AP Lit treats Streetcar as canonical American drama. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on illusion and reality often use it.

questions.prompt: 1950s San Francisco's Chinatown and pre-1949 China in The Joy Luck Club use this kind of split setting.
questions.explanation: Amy Tan's The Joy Luck Club (1989) alternates between mothers' pre-1949 China and daughters' 1980s San Francisco, dramatizing immigrant generational gaps. The dual setting is structural and thematic: it embodies the cultural translation problem at the novel's heart. RL.9-10.5 covers structural choices. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on identity, immigration, or family use this text. Common Core lists Tan as an exemplar of contemporary multicultural literature.

questions.prompt: The unnamed New England village in Shirley Jackson's 1948 story masks ritual violence with this surface quality.
questions.explanation: Shirley Jackson's 'The Lottery' (1948) sets ritual murder in an unnamed New England village on a 'clear and sunny' June morning. The setting's deliberate banality (children, gossip, weather) makes the climax horrifying. Jackson's craft demonstrates how setting can ironically subvert content. RL.9-10.4 covers tone; RL.9-10.5 covers structure. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on tradition or conformity often use this story. AP Lit analyzes its setting-content ironic gap.

questions.prompt: When an author tells us directly that 'Holden was 16 and unhappy,' this technique is used.
questions.explanation: Direct characterization explicitly states a character's traits through narration or other characters' statements. J.D. Salinger's The Catcher in the Rye (1951) uses Holden's first-person narration to directly characterize others ('Stradlater was a goddam liar'). RL.9-10.3 covers character development analysis. AP Lit demands students distinguish direct from indirect characterization and analyze why an author chose one. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests recognition; Part 3 essays require analysis.

questions.prompt: Revealing Holden's character through his actions, dialogue, and thoughts uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Indirect characterization reveals character through action, dialogue, thought, and others' reactions (the STEAL acronym: Speech, Thoughts, Effect on others, Actions, Looks). Salinger's Holden Caulfield characterizes himself through his obsessive language ('phony'), his protectiveness of Phoebe, his red hunting hat. RL.9-10.3 covers character analysis. AP Lit prizes precise STEAL analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on identity and character require this skill.

questions.prompt: Holden's red hunting hat and his constant lies make him this kind of layered protagonist.
questions.explanation: E.M. Forster's distinction between 'flat' (single trait) and 'round' (complex, contradictory) characters in Aspects of the Novel (1927) defines Holden as a round protagonist: protective brother, lying narrator, grieving Allie's death, contemptuous of phonies, vulnerable to Mr. Antolini. RL.9-10.3 requires analyzing how complex characters develop. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on adolescent identity often use Catcher. AP Lit demands precise complexity analysis.

questions.prompt: A character who changes significantly through the narrative, like Lennie's George after Lennie's death, is this.
questions.explanation: A dynamic character changes significantly through the narrative; a static character remains essentially unchanged. Brutus shifts from loyal friend to assassin to suicide. Blanche DuBois disintegrates from genteel arrival to mental collapse. RL.9-10.3 covers how characters develop over the course of a text. Distinct from round (complex) characters, dynamic refers to change across time. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on transformation and growth use this concept.

questions.prompt: Holden's running confession to readers, often unreliable, makes him this kind of narrator.
questions.explanation: An unreliable narrator's credibility is compromised. Holden's 'I'm the most terrific liar you ever saw in your life' confession three pages into Catcher signals readers to distrust his accounts. Wayne Booth coined the term in The Rhetoric of Fiction (1961). RL.9-10.6 covers point of view choices. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on truth and perception often involve unreliable narration. AP Lit demands precise POV analysis: unreliable vs limited vs omniscient.

questions.prompt: Piggy's glasses in Lord of the Flies primarily symbolize this human capacity.
questions.explanation: Golding makes Piggy's glasses the literal instrument of fire-making (focusing sunlight) and the symbolic instrument of reason on the island. When Jack steals them, he steals fire and rationality. The novel traces the boys' descent as the glasses pass from rightful use (signal fire) to dominance (Jack's hunting fire). RL.9-10.2 covers theme; RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats symbolism analysis as foundational. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments routinely test symbol recognition.

questions.prompt: The conch shell in Lord of the Flies, used to call meetings, symbolizes this concept.
questions.explanation: The conch shell summons assemblies and grants speakers the right to be heard. When Roger crushes it with the boulder in Chapter 11, Golding signals civilization's complete collapse. The conch is the novel's primary symbol of democratic order. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests symbol recognition. AP Lit requires students to argue not just 'X symbolizes Y' but 'how the symbol's transformation across the text develops theme.'.

questions.prompt: Holden's red hunting hat in The Catcher in the Rye symbolizes this aspect of his identity.
questions.explanation: Salinger makes Holden's red hunting hat a symbol of his individuality (it's loud, weird, his own) and his self-protection (he wears it backwards 'like a baseball catcher' when vulnerable). He gives it to Phoebe at the carousel, suggesting he can release his armor. RL.9-10.4 covers symbol. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on identity often use Catcher. AP Lit demands close attention to symbolic objects and what their fate reveals.

questions.prompt: Brutus's running on his own sword at Philippi marks this dramatic plot element.
questions.explanation: Catastrophe is the tragic resolution where the protagonist meets defeat or death, completing Aristotle's tragic arc. Brutus's suicide at Philippi (5.5) resolves the play with Antony's tribute 'This was the noblest Roman of them all.' RL.9-10.5 covers structural resolution. Aristotle's Poetics defines catastrophe as the necessary outcome of tragedy. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on tragedy or self-knowledge frequently use this ending. AP Lit demands precise structural vocabulary.

questions.prompt: The black box in Jackson's 'The Lottery' physically and symbolically represents this idea.
questions.explanation: Jackson describes the lottery's black box as worn, splintered, faded, repaired but never replaced because 'no one liked to upset even as much tradition as was represented by the black box.' The box embodies tradition's inertia: even when its origin is forgotten, the practice continues. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on tradition, conformity, or scapegoating routinely use 'The Lottery.' AP Lit treats it as a canonical short story.

questions.prompt: Ralph's democratic leadership in Lord of the Flies foils Jack's authoritarian impulse, illustrating this concept.
questions.explanation: A foil is a character whose traits contrast with another's to illuminate that character. Golding pairs Ralph (election, rules, signal fire) with Jack (hunting, force, war paint) to dramatize civilization versus savagery. RL.9-10.3 covers character analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on leadership or human nature use LOTF. AP Lit demands precise foil identification: not just 'X is a foil to Y,' but how X's specific traits highlight Y's specific traits.

questions.prompt: Stanley Kowalski's animalistic vitality foils Blanche DuBois's faded refinement, developing this theme.
questions.explanation: Williams's Streetcar pairs Stanley (Polish-American, working-class, sexual, vital) with Blanche (faded Southern belle, alcoholic, performative). Their conflict embodies post-WWII America displacing the antebellum South. RL.9-10.3 covers foils developing theme. AP Lit demands analysis of how foils dramatize ideological clashes. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on social change or class often use Streetcar. Williams's stage directions reinforce the contrast.

questions.prompt: Stella's name (Latin for star) in A Streetcar Named Desire suggests this thematic role.
questions.explanation: Tennessee Williams names Stella Kowalski after the Latin word for star, positioning her as Blanche's hopeful destination ('Stella for Star!' Blanche cries on arrival). The name foreshadows Stella's ultimate choice between her sister and her husband. Williams also names characters Blanche (white, blank), Stanley (stone, lithic), and Mitch (truncated). RL.9-10.4 covers word choice and figurative meaning. AP Lit prizes onomastic (name) analysis.

questions.prompt: Holden's lost brother Allie functions as foil to Holden, idealized as this kind of figure.
questions.explanation: Salinger makes Allie's memory (the left-handed catcher's mitt with poems written in green ink, dead of leukemia at 11) the foil to Holden's contaminated teenage world. Allie represents the innocence Holden tries to preserve. RL.9-10.3 covers character analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on grief or innocence often use Catcher. AP Lit demands recognition that absent characters can function as powerful foils.

questions.prompt: Okonkwo's rigid masculinity foils Obierika's reflective thoughtfulness in this Achebe novel.
questions.explanation: Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958) pairs Okonkwo (driven by fear of his father's weakness, performs hypermasculinity) with Obierika (Igbo philosopher, questions tradition, weeps openly). Obierika voices what Okonkwo cannot. RL.9-10.3 covers character. Achebe wrote partly in response to Joseph Conrad's Heart of Darkness; the foil dramatizes Igbo intellectual life. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on tradition and change often use this novel. AP Lit treats it as canonical postcolonial literature.

questions.prompt: A central insight about life or human nature in a text is called this.
choices: The challenge of maintaining humanity amid atrocity
questions.explanation: Theme is a complete statement of insight ('Power without principle corrupts the principled'), not a topic ('power'). RL.9-10.2 requires determining a theme and analyzing its development through specific details. Common Core distinguishes theme from summary. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice and Part 3 source-based argument essays both demand theme identification supported by textual evidence. AP Lit requires defending nuanced theme statements.

questions.prompt: Lord of the Flies' descent from rules to ritual murder develops this theme about human nature.
questions.explanation: Golding's Lord of the Flies traces how British schoolboys, removed from adult oversight, descend through stages: rules to face paint to hunting frenzy to Piggy's murder. The theme argues civilization is precariously held. RL.9-10.2 covers theme development. The novel's epigraph (Coral Island, R.M. Ballantyne) sets up Golding's deliberate inversion of Victorian optimism. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on human nature use LOTF. AP Lit treats it as canonical.

questions.prompt: Wiesel's Night develops this theme through young Eliezer's crisis of faith in Auschwitz.
choices: The challenge of maintaining humanity amid atrocity
questions.correctText: The challenge of maintaining humanity amid atrocity
questions.explanation: Wiesel's Night (1958) traces Eliezer's transformation from devout student to numb survivor: 'Never shall I forget that smoke. Never shall I forget the little faces of the children whose bodies I saw transformed into smoke under a silent sky.' The 'where is God now' moment at the hanging marks his crisis. RI.9-10.6 covers memoir; RL.9-10.2 covers theme. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the Holocaust and witness use this text.

questions.prompt: The Lord of the Flies, the pig's head on the stake, functions as this kind of moral abstraction.
choices: The challenge of maintaining humanity amid atrocity
questions.explanation: A symbol is a concrete thing that represents an abstract idea. Golding makes the Lord of the Flies (Beelzebub, fly-king, Hebrew for Satan) the pig's head on a stake that 'speaks' to Simon. The symbol externalizes evil as internal to humans. RL.9-10.4 covers symbol. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on human nature use LOTF. AP Lit demands precise symbol analysis: not just what X means, but how X's transformation across the text develops theme. Common Core lists symbol analysis as central.

questions.prompt: Catcher in the Rye develops this theme through Holden's obsession with the word 'phony.'.
choices: Authenticity in a fake adult world
questions.correctText: Authenticity in a fake adult world
questions.explanation: Salinger uses Holden's repeated 'phony' (45+ instances) to develop the theme that adult society demands a kind of fakeness Holden cannot accept. The catcher fantasy (catching children before they fall off the cliff into adulthood) crystallizes the theme. RL.9-10.2 covers theme. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on adolescent identity use Catcher. AP Lit prizes precise diction-theme analysis. The novel was the most-banned book in 1973-78.

questions.prompt: Shakespeare's 14-line poem with abab cdcd efef gg rhyme scheme is called this kind of sonnet.
questions.explanation: The Shakespearean (English) sonnet has 14 lines of iambic pentameter rhyming abab cdcd efef gg, structured as three quatrains and a closing couplet. Surrey developed it; Shakespeare perfected 154 of them. RL.9-10.5 covers form-meaning relationships. Sonnet 18 ('Shall I compare thee to a summer's day') is canonical. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests sonnet recognition. AP Lit requires identifying sonnet types and analyzing how form supports meaning. Distinct from the Petrarchan (Italian) sonnet.

questions.prompt: The Italian (Petrarchan) sonnet divides into an 8-line stanza and this final 6-line stanza.
questions.explanation: The Petrarchan sonnet has an octave (8 lines, abbaabba) and a sestet (6 lines, varied: cdcdcd or cdecde). The volta (turn) appears between octave and sestet, shifting argument or feeling. Petrarch perfected it for his Laura sonnets. Milton, Wordsworth, and Edna St. Vincent Millay wrote Petrarchan sonnets. RL.9-10.5 covers structural choices. AP Lit demands precise sonnet-type recognition and analysis of volta function. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test sonnet form basics.

questions.prompt: The closing couplet in a Shakespearean sonnet often does this rhetorical work.
questions.explanation: The Shakespearean sonnet's closing couplet (lines 13-14) typically resolves the argument, delivers a witty turn, or undermines the previous twelve lines. Sonnet 18 closes 'So long as men can breathe or eyes can see, / So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.' RL.9-10.5 covers form-meaning. AP Lit prizes couplet analysis: does it resolve, ironize, deepen, or undermine? Regents Part 3 source-based arguments may use sonnets. The volta in Shakespearean form often sits at the couplet.

questions.prompt: The metrical pattern of unstressed-stressed syllables in five pairs per line is called this.
questions.explanation: Iambic pentameter is the meter of English verse: five iambs per line, each iamb being an unstressed-stressed syllable pair (de-DUM de-DUM de-DUM de-DUM de-DUM). 'Shall I com-PARE thee TO a SUM-mer's DAY?' Shakespeare uses it in sonnets and plays. RL.9-10.5 covers form. AP Lit requires scansion (marking meter) and analysis of metrical variation. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test iamb recognition. Substitutions (trochees, spondees) often signal emphasis.

questions.prompt: Petrarchan sonnets shift their argument or emotion at this structural point between octave and sestet.
questions.explanation: The volta (Italian for 'turn') marks the shift in argument, emotion, or perspective in a sonnet. In Petrarchan sonnets, the volta typically sits between octave and sestet (line 9). In Shakespearean sonnets, the volta can appear at line 9 or shift to the closing couplet (line 13). RL.9-10.5 covers form-meaning relationships. AP Lit demands precise volta identification and analysis. Regents Part 2 may test volta recognition. The volta is the sonnet's structural hinge.

questions.prompt: Holden Caulfield narrating his New York weekend uses this point of view.
questions.explanation: Salinger uses first-person narration in Catcher: 16-year-old Holden tells his story directly to readers from a sanitarium ('I'll just tell you about this madman stuff that happened to me'). First-person creates intimacy and limits readers to the narrator's perspective. RL.9-10.6 covers point of view. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests POV. AP Lit demands deeper analysis: how does first-person create dramatic irony, sympathy, or limitation? Holden's POV combined with his unreliability creates layered meaning.

questions.prompt: The Joy Luck Club rotating among four mothers and four daughters uses this POV structure.
questions.explanation: Amy Tan's The Joy Luck Club (1989) uses eight first-person narrators (four mother-daughter pairs) across sixteen chapters, structured by the mahjong table's four winds. The polyphonic structure embodies the novel's theme of cultural translation and generational gaps. RL.9-10.5 covers structural choices; RL.9-10.6 covers POV. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on immigration or family use this novel. AP Lit treats multivocal narration as a sophisticated structural choice.

questions.prompt: Lord of the Flies' narrator who knows all the boys' thoughts uses this POV.
questions.explanation: Golding uses third-person omniscient narration in Lord of the Flies, accessing Ralph's, Jack's, Piggy's, and Simon's thoughts. Omniscience lets Golding develop multiple character arcs simultaneously. RL.9-10.6 covers POV. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on collective behavior use LOTF. AP Lit demands recognition that omniscient narrators may be 'editorial' (commenting) or 'neutral' (just reporting). Distinguishing omniscient from limited POV is foundational.

questions.prompt: Things Fall Apart's narrator who follows Okonkwo's perspective closely without entering his thoughts uses this POV.
questions.explanation: Achebe uses third-person limited narration in Things Fall Apart, focalized mostly through Okonkwo. We see his actions but rarely his inner thoughts, creating ironic distance. The limitation lets Achebe critique Okonkwo while letting readers feel his weight. RL.9-10.6 covers POV. AP Lit prizes precise focalization analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on colonialism and tradition use this text. Limited POV creates dramatic irony and sympathy.

questions.prompt: Jackson's 'The Lottery' narrator who simply reports actions without entering any character's mind uses this POV.
questions.explanation: Third-person objective (dramatic) narration reports only external action without entering any character's thoughts, like a camera. Jackson's 'The Lottery' uses near-objective narration to delay revelation of the ritual's purpose. Hemingway's 'Hills Like White Elephants' is canonical objective POV. RL.9-10.6 covers POV. AP Lit prizes objective analysis: what does the writer's refusal to enter consciousness create? Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests POV identification.

questions.prompt: Sonnet 18's 'Shall I compare thee to a summer's day' uses this device of explicit comparison.
questions.explanation: A simile compares unlike things using 'like' or 'as' (or 'shall I compare,' 'than,' 'as if'). Sonnet 18 opens with a simile-like rhetorical question, then develops into extended comparison. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests simile recognition; AP Lit demands precise distinction from metaphor and explanation of why the writer chose simile. Sonnet 18 uses similes and metaphors to argue poetry preserves beauty beyond seasonal change.

questions.prompt: Stanley Kowalski's 'I am the king around here!' uses this device of overstated assertion.
questions.explanation: Hyperbole is intentional exaggeration for emphasis or effect. Stanley's 'I am the king around here!' (Streetcar, scene 8) declares dominance through hyperbole. Williams uses hyperbole for Stanley's territorial assertion and Blanche's romantic delusions ('I want magic!'). RL.9-10.4 covers figurative language. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests hyperbole recognition. AP Lit and AP Lang demand analysis of how exaggeration serves character or argument. Distinct from litotes (understatement) and metaphor (substitution).

questions.prompt: Williams's 'streetcar named Desire' transferring to 'Cemeteries' uses this device of literal-into-symbolic.
questions.explanation: Williams takes literal New Orleans streetcar names (Desire, Cemeteries, Elysian Fields) and uses them symbolically to map Blanche's psychological journey: Desire (her past disgrace) to Cemeteries (her dead family) to Elysian Fields (her arrival at Stella's). RL.9-10.4 covers figurative language. AP Lit prizes symbolic detail analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on illusion and reality use Streetcar. Williams's symbol-saturated drama rewards close reading.

questions.prompt: Achebe weaving Igbo proverbs into Things Fall Apart's English prose uses this cross-cultural technique.
questions.explanation: Achebe writes in English but threads Igbo proverbs ('Among the Ibo the art of conversation is regarded very highly, and proverbs are the palm-oil with which words are eaten'), folk tales, and Igbo terms. The hybrid style asserts African intellectual life against colonial dismissal. RL.9-10.4 covers diction and style. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on cultural identity use this text. AP Lit and postcolonial criticism prize Achebe's hybrid style as foundational.

questions.prompt: Sonnet 18 closing 'So long lives this, and this gives life to thee' uses this device of self-reference.
questions.explanation: Metapoetics is poetry that refers to itself or to the act of writing. Sonnet 18's closing couplet ('So long as men can breathe or eyes can see, / So long lives this, and this gives life to thee') refers to the sonnet itself as the preservation mechanism. Shakespeare's argument: this very poem outlasts mortal beauty. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative language; RL.9-10.5 covers form. AP Lit demands recognition of metapoetic moves. Sonnet 55 makes the argument explicit: 'Not marble, nor the gilded monuments of princes shall outlive this powerful rhyme.'.

questions.prompt: The recurring image of birds (parakeets, jays) in The Joy Luck Club functions as this craft element.
questions.explanation: Motifs are recurring images, ideas, or elements that develop theme. Tan threads birds, mahjong, and food across The Joy Luck Club. The mothers' bird stories (the swan, the moon lady, etc.) connect Chinese to American chapters. RL.9-10.2 covers theme development through specific details; motifs are prime vehicles. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the immigrant experience use this novel. AP Lit demands motif tracking across the whole text, not just identification in one passage.

questions.prompt: Sandra Cisneros's short numbered vignettes in The House on Mango Street form this structural choice.
questions.explanation: Cisneros's The House on Mango Street (1984) uses 44 short vignettes (often a page or two) to narrate Esperanza's Chicago coming-of-age. The form mirrors childhood memory's fragmentary nature and lets Cisneros write in poetic prose. RL.9-10.5 covers structural choices. The book is canonical in Common Core curricula. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on identity, place, or coming-of-age use this text. AP Lit treats vignette structure as a sophisticated formal choice.

questions.prompt: Wiesel's stripped-down sentences in Night ('Never shall I forget...') use this technique for emotional weight.
questions.explanation: Anaphora repeats words or phrases at the start of successive sentences or clauses. Wiesel's 'Never shall I forget that night, the first night in camp. Never shall I forget that smoke. Never shall I forget the little faces...' (7 'Never shall I forget' in one passage) creates incantatory weight. RI.9-10.6 and RL.9-10.4 both cover figurative and rhetorical analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on Holocaust or memory use Night. AP Lang treats it as canonical.

questions.prompt: Things Fall Apart develops this theme through Okonkwo's pre-colonial Igbo society's transformation.
questions.explanation: Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958) traces Okonkwo's Umuofia clan as British colonizers and missionaries arrive in 1890s Nigeria. The title (from Yeats's 'The Second Coming') signals the theme: structures hold, then fail. Achebe wrote against Conrad's Heart of Darkness portrayal of Africa. RL.9-10.2 covers theme. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on cultural change or colonialism use this novel. AP Lit treats Achebe as a foundational postcolonial author.

questions.prompt: Jackson's deliberate misdirection in 'The Lottery' delaying revelation of ritual murder uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Jackson withholds the lottery's purpose until the final pages, describing 'pieces of paper' and 'slips' without naming consequence. The strategic ambiguity makes the reveal devastating. The story produced furious reader mail when The New Yorker published it (June 26, 1948). RL.9-10.5 covers structural choices and effects. AP Lit prizes recognition of strategic ambiguity as a craft element. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on tradition or scapegoating use this story.

questions.prompt: The single most important point of an informational text is called this.
questions.explanation: Main idea (central idea) is the overarching point of an informational text, supported by details, examples, and evidence. RI.9-10.2 explicitly asks students to determine a central idea and analyze its development. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests with 'Which sentence best states the central idea?' Part 3 source-based argument essays require synthesizing central ideas across multiple texts. Distinct from a topic (one or two words); a main idea is a complete sentence asserting a position.

questions.prompt: The author's arguable, defensible main position is called this in argumentation.
questions.explanation: A claim is the author's arguable position. RI.9-10.6 examines purpose; RI.9-10.8 analyzes claims. Stephen Toulmin's argument model (claim, data, warrant, backing, rebuttal, qualifier) underlies most claim analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays require identifying claims across sources and forming the student's own. AP Lang's argument essay rubric weighs claim quality heavily. Strong claims are debatable; mere facts and topics are not claims. Distinguishing claim from topic is foundational.

questions.prompt: The opposing view the writer must acknowledge in argumentative writing is this.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is the opposing position a writer addresses to strengthen the main argument. W.9-10.1b explicitly requires developing 'claim(s) and counterclaims fairly.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly evaluate counterclaim acknowledgment. AP Lang argument essays heavily reward genuine counterclaim engagement. The strongest arguments concede something to the counterclaim before refuting it. Refusing to engage signals weak argument and ego-driven writing.

questions.prompt: Specific facts, examples, statistics, and quotations that prove a main idea are called these.
questions.explanation: Supporting details are the specific facts, examples, anecdotes, statistics, and quotations that prove a main idea. RI.9-10.1 requires citing strong and thorough textual evidence; RI.9-10.2 analyzes how details develop central ideas. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests detail-main-idea relationships. Part 3 source-based argument essays demand specific details, not paraphrased generalities. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays both rate evidence specificity heavily.

questions.prompt: Arguing 'this expert says X, so X must be true' may commit this fallacy when the expert is outside their field.
questions.explanation: Appeal to false authority (or inappropriate authority) cites an expert outside their area of expertise. 'Einstein opposed the atomic bomb, so we shouldn't use nuclear energy.' RI.9-10.8 covers evaluating argument. Distinct from legitimate appeal to expertise (citing a relevant expert is sound). AP Lang argument essays explicitly score fallacy recognition. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays may plant this fallacy for student detection. Critical thinking depends on expert-domain matching.

questions.prompt: A claim asserting 'X is harmful' or 'X is just' is this kind of claim.
questions.explanation: A claim of value judges merit, ethics, or aesthetics. King's letter argues a claim of value: 'There are just and there are unjust laws.' RI.9-10.8 distinguishes claim types. Toulmin's framework and Aristotelian rhetoric both classify claims by type. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays frequently pose value questions ('Is technology helping or harming teens?'). AP Lang argument essays often require defending value claims with evidence and reasoning. Distinct from fact claims (verifiable) and policy claims (actionable).

questions.prompt: When a writer makes a sweeping claim based on a single example, this fallacy occurs.
questions.explanation: Hasty generalization draws a broad conclusion from insufficient evidence. 'My uncle smoked his whole life and lived to 95, so smoking isn't dangerous.' RI.9-10.8 requires evaluating reasoning and evidence sufficiency. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test recognition. AP Lang argument essays penalize hasty generalization in student writing and reward identification in source analysis. One anecdote rarely supports a universal claim. Distinct from a representative example (which is calibrated).

questions.prompt: Asserting that X happened because of Y is this kind of claim.
questions.explanation: A claim of cause asserts that X causes Y (or Y is the effect of X). Causal claims require evidence beyond correlation: temporal sequence, mechanism, ruling out alternatives. RI.9-10.8 evaluates reasoning. Post hoc ergo propter hoc (assuming sequence implies causation) is a common causal fallacy. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments often hinge on causal claims. AP Lang argument essays explicitly stamp out fallacious causation. Strong causal claims acknowledge complexity, multiple causes, and counterexamples.

questions.prompt: An argument is sound only if its evidence is relevant and this in quantity.
questions.explanation: Evidence sufficiency means enough evidence supports the claim. A single example may suffice for an existence claim ('there is at least one X') but fails for a universal claim ('all X are Y'). RI.9-10.8 explicitly requires evaluating whether reasoning is valid and evidence is relevant and sufficient. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays evaluate evidence quality. AP Lang argument essays score sophistication partly through evidence proportion: enough specific examples to convince without overload.

questions.prompt: Asserting 'X means Y' or 'X is best classified as Z' is this kind of claim.
questions.explanation: A claim of definition asserts what something is or how to classify it ('Civil disobedience is not lawlessness'). Definitional claims often underlie larger arguments: define the contested term first. RI.9-10.4 covers determining meaning; RI.9-10.8 evaluates argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often hinge on contested definitions. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays require precise definitional work. Aristotle's classification logic underlies definitional argument.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's appeal to logic, reasoning, and evidence is called this.
questions.explanation: Logos is Aristotle's appeal to logic, supported by facts, statistics, examples, and valid inference. From Rhetoric (Book 1), Aristotle's three artistic proofs are ethos, pathos, and logos. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise identification of logos: was evidence empirical, was syllogism valid? Quantitative data, expert testimony, and historical precedent all serve logos.

questions.prompt: An argument appealing to readers' grief, anger, or fear draws on this Aristotelian appeal.
questions.explanation: Pathos is Aristotle's appeal to emotion, mobilizing pity, anger, fear, hope, or pride. Wiesel's Night uses pathos to make Holocaust history viscerally felt. King's Birmingham letter describes 'when you have seen vicious mobs lynch your mothers and fathers' to evoke moral outrage. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical analysis. Regents Part 3 and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise emotion-naming, not just 'this is emotional.' Strong analysis names what emotion is evoked and how.

questions.prompt: Briefly granting an opposing point's validity before refuting it is this rhetorical move.
questions.explanation: Concession acknowledges the opposing view's merit before reasserting the writer's position. King concedes in Birmingham letter that some laws should be obeyed but argues unjust laws must be resisted. W.9-10.1b requires fair counterclaim development. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward strategic concession. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score concession-and-refutation as sophistication markers. Refusing concession signals weak rhetoric and damages ethos.

questions.prompt: The speaker's authority and credibility, in Aristotle's framework, is called this.
questions.explanation: Ethos is Aristotle's appeal based on speaker character and credibility. King establishes ethos through his pastoral signature, theological quotations, and lived experience of civil rights. Wiesel's ethos rests on Auschwitz survivorship. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both routinely test ethos identification and effect. Strong ethos: authority, expertise, character. Failed ethos: hypocrisy, vagueness, lack of credentials.

questions.prompt: Arguing 'we've always done it this way' commits this fallacy of unexamined custom.
questions.explanation: Appeal to tradition (argumentum ad antiquitatem) assumes that because something has been done a certain way, it should continue. Shirley Jackson's 'The Lottery' literalizes this fallacy: Old Man Warner says 'Lottery in June, corn be heavy soon.' RI.9-10.8 covers fallacies. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score fallacy recognition. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays may test this fallacy in source texts. Distinct from precedent (which is reasoned tradition with stated justification).

questions.prompt: A scholar's analysis of MLK's speeches, written decades later, is this kind of source.
questions.explanation: Secondary sources analyze, interpret, or report on primary sources after the fact: textbooks, scholarly articles, biographies. A historian writing about MLK uses primary speeches and creates secondary work. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view; RI.9-10.7 analyzes accounts across mediums. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays mix source types; evaluation requires distinction. AP Lang argument essays require source-type awareness. Common Core's research strand emphasizes both types.

questions.prompt: A study about teen behavior from 1995 may suffer from this credibility weakness for current claims.
questions.explanation: Currency is one of five CRAAP-test source-evaluation criteria (Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, Purpose). Outdated data on rapidly changing topics (technology, public health, social trends) misleads. RI.9-10.8 covers source evaluation. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays may include outdated statistics. AP Lang demands source vetting. W.9-10.8 explicitly covers source evaluation. A 1995 teen study might still be relevant for some claims (basic development) but not others (social media).

questions.prompt: Eyewitness testimony, original documents, and primary records are this kind of source.
questions.explanation: Primary sources originate from the time and place of the event: diaries, letters, speeches, photographs, government records, interviews. Wiesel's Night functions as a primary source on the Holocaust. RI.9-10.1 requires citing strong textual evidence. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly mix primary and secondary sources; identifying which is which unlocks evaluation. AP US History training also emphasizes this distinction. Common Core's research strand requires source-type awareness.

questions.prompt: A peer-reviewed scientific journal article has this strength compared to a blog post.
questions.explanation: Peer-reviewed scholarly sources have undergone expert evaluation before publication, providing authority that blog posts lack. RI.9-10.8 evaluates source authority. W.9-10.8 covers gathering credible sources. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes mix scholarly and popular sources for student evaluation. AP Lang's synthesis essay (formerly part of the exam) explicitly required source-type evaluation. Not all peer review is equal: predatory journals exist. Scholarly authority is a starting point, not absolute.

questions.prompt: A source claiming 'studies show' without naming the studies commits this evidence weakness.
questions.explanation: Unverifiable evidence (vague attribution to 'studies' or 'experts' without naming them) prevents fact-checking. 'Studies show' is a red flag in argument analysis. RI.9-10.8 requires evaluating evidence. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays may include sources guilty of this weakness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis trains students to flag unverifiable evidence as a rhetorical move. Strong writing names sources specifically; weak writing hides behind anonymous authority.

questions.prompt: A claim asserting that something 'should be done' is this kind of claim.
questions.explanation: A claim of policy advocates a specific action ('Schools should adopt later start times'). Policy claims build on fact claims (problem exists) and value claims (problem is bad) to recommend action. RI.9-10.8 covers argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pose policy questions. AP Lang argument essays frequently require policy positions. The structure problem-cause-solution organizes most policy arguments. Strong policy claims address feasibility, not just desirability.

questions.prompt: A source whose unstated political agenda shapes its conclusions has this problem.
questions.explanation: Bias is a source's unstated preference, shaped by funding, political affiliation, or personal investment. A tobacco-industry-funded smoking study has structural bias. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view; RI.9-10.8 evaluates argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes pair biased and neutral sources. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays reward bias-aware source handling. Bias detection requires examining: who funded this, who published it, what perspective is missing?

questions.prompt: Directly disproving an opposing view through evidence and reasoning is this argument move.
questions.explanation: Refutation disproves the opposing view through evidence, logical analysis, or fallacy exposure. King's letter refutes the clergy's 'unwise and untimely' charges by exposing false dichotomy between order and justice. W.9-10.1b covers counterclaim development. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward refutation. AP Lang argument essays rate refutation quality. Effective refutation engages the opposing argument's strongest version, not a straw-man weakening of it.

questions.prompt: Toulmin's argument model names the underlying reasoning connecting evidence to claim as this.
questions.explanation: In Stephen Toulmin's argument model (The Uses of Argument, 1958), the warrant is the underlying assumption connecting data (evidence) to claim. If the claim is 'cars should be electric' and the data is 'electric cars emit less carbon,' the warrant is 'we should reduce carbon emissions.' RI.9-10.8 requires evaluating reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward explicit warrants. AP Lang argument writing demands warrant articulation. Implicit warrants weaken arguments.

questions.prompt: Structuring an essay 'state position, address opposition, restate position' creates this organizational pattern.
questions.explanation: Classical argument structure (from Aristotle and Cicero) has five parts: introduction (exordium), narration of facts (narratio), confirmation of claim (confirmatio), refutation of opposition (refutatio), conclusion (peroratio). W.9-10.1 covers argumentative writing. AP Lang argument essays accept classical structure; rhetorical analysis essays often analyze it in source speeches. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly use this structure. Mastery of classical structure unlocks both analysis and writing.

questions.prompt: Wiesel's Night narrator describing his transformation in clipped, restrained language has this tone.
questions.explanation: Restrained tone conveys intense feeling through deliberate understatement. Wiesel's prose ('I had ceased to be anything but ashes') uses restraint to make horror unbearable. Compare with hyperbolic prose, which would diminish impact. RI.9-10.4 covers tone analysis. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests precise tone naming. AP Lang and AP Lit demand sophisticated tone vocabulary: not 'serious,' but 'restrained' or 'austere.' Distinct from cold (which signals lack of feeling) or muted (which signals partial expression).

questions.prompt: A speaker openly mourning loss with grief-saturated language has this tone.
questions.explanation: Elegiac tone laments loss, like Antony's 'O mighty Caesar! Dost thou lie so low?' (Julius Caesar 3.1). Elegiac writing (the elegy tradition: Milton's Lycidas, Shelley's Adonais, Whitman's 'When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd') mourns specifically. RI.9-10.4 covers tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests advanced tone vocabulary. AP Lit demands precise tone vocabulary, especially for elegiac, threnodic, or lamenting prose. Wiesel's restrained tone differs from elegiac tone (which is more openly grieving).

questions.prompt: A speaker expressing barely suppressed outrage at injustice has this tone.
questions.explanation: Indignant tone conveys righteous anger at wrongdoing. Frederick Douglass's 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852) reaches indignant peaks: 'This Fourth of July is yours, not mine.' RI.9-10.4 examines tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests precise tone naming. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays demand distinguishing indignant (righteous anger) from angry (unspecified rage) or bitter (resentful)—a precise tone vocabulary unlocks rhetorical analysis.

questions.prompt: Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' to eat Irish babies demonstrates this tone of biting irony.
questions.explanation: Satirical tone uses irony, exaggeration, and ridicule to expose folly or vice. Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) deadpan-suggests selling Irish babies for food to wealthy English. Orwell's Animal Farm employs satirical tone in its omniscient narration. RI.9-10.4 covers tone. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often include satirical passages. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays require satire identification and analysis of its rhetorical purpose (often exposing power).

questions.prompt: A writer combining scorn with bitter humor uses this advanced tone.
questions.explanation: Sardonic tone combines scorn with bitter humor; it mocks while smiling. Orwell's 1984 narrator describing Big Brother is sardonic. Holden Caulfield's narration alternates sardonic and elegiac tones (his dismissals of the 'phony' world; his memory of Allie). RI.9-10.4 covers tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests advanced tone vocabulary. AP Lit and AP Lang demand precise tone names: sardonic, wry, acerbic, jocular. Avoid 'serious' or 'funny' generically.

questions.prompt: Wiesel writing Night to bear witness to the Holocaust serves primarily this purpose.
questions.explanation: Author's purpose can be to inform, persuade, entertain, or express. Wiesel's Night (1958) bears witness, a hybrid persuasive-expressive purpose: he persuades readers to remember and never to allow recurrence. RI.9-10.6 explicitly covers purpose. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests purpose identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays must explain how the writer's choices advance the specific purpose. Distinct from informational (just facts) and entertaining (storytelling for pleasure).

questions.prompt: An article explaining how an ecosystem works has primarily this purpose.
questions.explanation: Informational/expository purpose is to explain or convey knowledge clearly without arguing a position. Science writing, encyclopedia entries, technical manuals, and most news reporting are primarily informational. RI.9-10.6 covers purpose. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests purpose categorization. AP Lang requires distinguishing informational from argumentative (taking a position) and from expressive (writer's emotions or experience). Common Core distinguishes the genres for both reading and writing strands.

questions.prompt: Aristotle defined this purpose as creating pleasurable emotional release through art.
questions.explanation: Aristotle's Poetics defines tragedy's purpose as catharsis: the purging of pity and fear through art. Modern entertainment purpose includes storytelling for pleasure, comedy, suspense, and aesthetic experience. RI.9-10.6 covers purpose. RL.9-10 standards treat aesthetic purpose as central to literature. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests purpose. AP Lit examines how aesthetic and emotional purpose drive literary craft. Distinct from didactic literature (which teaches) or propagandistic art (which persuades).

questions.prompt: Joan Didion's personal essays meditating on California illustrate this purpose.
questions.explanation: Expressive purpose centers the writer's perspective, feelings, or experience, often in personal essays or memoirs. Didion's 'Slouching Towards Bethlehem' (1968) and 'The Year of Magical Thinking' (2005) blend reportage with personal reflection. RI.9-10.6 covers purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays occasionally include literary nonfiction. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays draw on expressive writing as evidence. Expressive writing often combines purposes: expressing personal experience to inform or persuade readers.

questions.prompt: A writer arguing 'we should adopt the four-day school week' has this primary purpose.
questions.explanation: Advocating policy is a specific persuasive purpose: arguing for or against a course of action. Distinct from informing (just facts) or expressing (just experience), policy advocacy combines fact, value, and policy claims to recommend action. W.9-10.1 covers argument writing; RI.9-10.6 covers reading purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pose policy questions. AP Lang argument essays explicitly include policy advocacy as a common task. Strong policy arguments address feasibility.

questions.prompt: King's repeated 'I have a dream' at the start of successive clauses uses this device.
questions.explanation: Anaphora repeats a word or phrase at the start of successive clauses. MLK's 'I have a dream that one day...' (8 repetitions) and Lincoln's 'we cannot dedicate, we cannot consecrate, we cannot hallow' are canonical. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise anaphora identification. Distinguish from epistrophe (ending repetition) and symploke (both). Anaphora creates rhythm and emphasis.

questions.prompt: Speech analysis acronym SOAPSTone stands for Speaker, Occasion, Audience, Purpose, Subject, and this.
questions.explanation: SOAPSTone (Speaker, Occasion, Audience, Purpose, Subject, Tone) is a College Board-developed rhetorical analysis tool. It forces students to map every element of the rhetorical situation before analyzing devices. RI.9-10.6 explicitly covers point of view and purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require SOAPSTone thinking. AP Lang training builds rhetorical analysis on this framework. Knowing SOAPSTone unlocks any speech or essay.

questions.prompt: JFK's 'Ask not what your country can do for you—ask what you can do for your country' uses this device.
questions.explanation: Chiasmus reverses the order of words in parallel clauses (ABBA pattern). JFK's 1961 inaugural is canonical. Mae West: 'It's not the men in my life, it's the life in my men.' RI.9-10.6 covers advanced rhetorical figures. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise device-naming. Chiasmus differs from antimetabole (which requires exact word reversal); some critics use them interchangeably. The cross-pattern is memorable and quotable.

questions.prompt: MLK's 'I have a dream that my four little children will one day live in a nation' uses this device.
questions.explanation: Personalization makes abstract claims concrete by anchoring them in specific people or experiences. King's reference to 'my four little children' shifts civil rights from abstract principle to personal stake. The technique builds pathos and ethos simultaneously. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical strategy. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward concrete examples. AP Lang argument essays score evidence specificity. Personalization is foundational to effective rhetoric across genres.

questions.prompt: A writer using 'you' to address readers directly creates this kind of rhetorical relationship.
questions.explanation: Direct address ('you' to the reader) creates intimacy and pulls the reader into the argument. James Baldwin's 'My Dungeon Shook' (1963) addresses his nephew directly, modeling how second-person address makes general arguments personal. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical strategy. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes feature second-person texts. AP Lang demands students analyze pronoun choice. Distinguish direct address from apostrophe (addressing the absent or abstract).

questions.prompt: Repeating a word at the end of successive clauses, like 'in 1492, in 1776, in 1865' all ending with key terms, uses this.
questions.explanation: Epistrophe (Greek: 'turning back') repeats words at the end of successive clauses. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address 'government of the people, by the people, for the people' uses repetition of 'the people.' RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise device-naming. Combined anaphora and epistrophe is symploke ('When there is need of silence, you speak, and when there is need of speech, you remain silent').

questions.prompt: The relationship between speech context, audience, and purpose is called the rhetorical this.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical situation (Lloyd Bitzer, 1968) includes exigence (urgent issue), audience (those who can act), and constraints (factors limiting what can be said). Antony's funeral oration occurs in a specific situation (Caesar dead, mob present, conspirators armed) shaping what he can say. RI.9-10.6 covers context-driven rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require situational awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis demands explicit situational analysis.

questions.prompt: King's audience for 'I Have a Dream' included this group beyond the 250,000 at the Lincoln Memorial.
questions.explanation: King's August 28, 1963 speech reached 250,000 at the Lincoln Memorial plus a national TV audience watching live coverage of the March on Washington. The dual audience shaped King's rhetorical choices: he addressed both committed civil rights activists and watching white moderates. RI.9-10.6 covers audience analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand audience-aware analysis. King's rhetoric balances multiple audiences simultaneously.

questions.prompt: Marc Antony's 'And Brutus is an honorable man' repeated with growing irony uses this device.
choices: The challenge of maintaining humanity amid atrocity
questions.explanation: Verbal irony means the opposite of what is said. Antony's funeral oration in Julius Caesar 3.2 repeats 'Brutus is an honourable man' with rising sarcasm until the crowd hears it as accusation. RI.9-10.6 and RL.9-10.4 both cover irony. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests irony types: verbal (saying opposite), situational (event-outcome mismatch), dramatic (audience knows what character does not). AP Lit rewards precise irony analysis and effect explanation.

questions.prompt: The original context that prompts a speech, like Caesar's assassination prompting Antony's oration, is this.
questions.explanation: Exigence (Lloyd Bitzer's term) is the urgent issue or event that calls forth rhetoric. Antony's exigence: Caesar's body lies before a crowd, the conspirators speak first, Rome's future is in the balance. RI.9-10.6 covers context. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require exigence awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays explicitly score exigence identification as part of rhetorical situation analysis. Exigence shapes purpose and constraints.

questions.prompt: A writer's openly stated position is called this kind of stance.
questions.explanation: An explicit stance is openly stated; an implicit stance is suggested through tone, evidence selection, and word choice. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. Skilled writers may take an implicit stance (Animal Farm's anti-Stalinism) or an explicit one (King's anti-segregation). Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests stance identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays often examine implicit stances in nonfiction prose where the writer doesn't directly state the claim.

questions.prompt: The triangular relationship of writer, audience, and subject is called the rhetorical this.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical triangle (Aristotle) connects writer/speaker (ethos), audience (pathos), and subject (logos). Effective rhetoric balances all three. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pose questions about audience or speaker stance. AP Lang's rhetorical analysis essay requires mapping the triangle: who is speaking, to whom, about what, and how the writer's choices serve all three points.

questions.prompt: A writer treating both sides fairly without clear position has this stance.
questions.explanation: Objective stance presents information without favoring a side. Encyclopedic and most news writing aim for objectivity. RI.9-10.6 covers stance. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests stance categorization. AP Lang requires distinguishing objective (neutral) from subjective (personal-view-laden) writing. True objectivity is contested; writers' choices about what to include shape stance even without explicit position. The distinction is foundational for source evaluation.

questions.prompt: A writer using 'I,' personal anecdote, and emotional language adopts this stance.
questions.explanation: Subjective stance foregrounds the writer's personal perspective. Joan Didion, James Baldwin, and Wiesel write subjectively while making larger arguments. RI.9-10.6 covers stance. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pair subjective and objective sources. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays must integrate both stances strategically; rhetorical analysis must name when and why a writer is subjective. Subjective stance is not weakness but rhetorical choice for specific effect.

questions.prompt: An argument that grants the reader's likely position before contesting it builds this rhetorical quality.
questions.explanation: Goodwill (Aristotle's eunoia) is the rhetorical impression that the writer cares about the audience's wellbeing. Concession to opposing views, fair tone, and audience-aware diction build goodwill. Strong arguments earn reader trust before pressing claims. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward writing that builds goodwill. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score audience awareness. Often invisible to weak readers but powerful in skilled hands.

questions.prompt: An arguable, specific, defensible statement of the writer's position is called this.
questions.explanation: A thesis is the writer's specific, arguable, defensible position. W.9-10.1a requires introducing precise claims. Strong theses are debatable, specific, and forecast organization. Weak: 'Romeo and Juliet is interesting.' Strong: 'Williams's Streetcar uses Stanley and Blanche to argue that postwar American vitality crushes the antebellum South's lingering refinement.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score thesis quality heavily. AP Lang argument essays require thesis identification.

questions.prompt: A thesis must be this quality to function as the foundation of an argument essay.
questions.explanation: Arguability distinguishes a thesis from a topic statement. 'World War II started in 1939' is factual, not arguable. 'World War II's primary cause was the Treaty of Versailles, not Hitler's personal ambition' is arguable. W.9-10.1a requires writers to introduce 'precise claim(s).' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward arguable theses. AP Lang's argument essay rubric distinguishes defensible thesis (1 pt) from no thesis (0 pts).

questions.prompt: Acknowledging 'although critics argue X' before stating the claim creates this sophisticated thesis structure.
questions.explanation: A concessive thesis begins with 'Although critics argue X...' before stating the writer's position. The structure demonstrates argument awareness and sophistication. Example: 'Although some readers view Atticus as a savior figure, Lee's portrayal more honestly positions him as a flawed but principled lawyer.' W.9-10.1b requires fair counterclaim development. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward this structure.

questions.prompt: A thesis that forecasts the essay's main supporting points uses this kind of structure.
questions.explanation: A mapped thesis previews the essay's structure by listing main supporting points. 'Shakespeare uses dramatic irony, foreshadowing, and parallel structure to develop tragedy.' The structure aids organization. W.9-10.1a covers claim introduction. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often reward mapped thesis. AP Lang argument essays accept mapped thesis but reward more sophisticated structures. Avoid over-mechanical mapping that constrains argument.

questions.prompt: A weak thesis that just restates the prompt or asserts an obvious fact has this flaw.
questions.explanation: Vague theses ('This essay will discuss themes in TKAM') fail because they neither argue nor specify. Strong theses name the text, the specific claim, and often the mechanism. W.9-10.1a explicitly requires 'precise claim(s).' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays penalize thesis vagueness. AP Lang's argument essay rubric awards thesis points only for defensible, specific claims. Students should ask: would anyone disagree with my thesis? If not, it's not a thesis.

questions.prompt: Addressing 'they say' before 'I say' in argumentative writing creates this structure.
questions.explanation: Graff and Birkenstein's 'They Say / I Say' framework structures arguments as conversation. W.9-10.1b explicitly requires developing 'claim(s) and counterclaims fairly.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays require counterclaim engagement. AP Lang argument essays score counterargument quality. The structure builds ethos by showing the writer has considered the other side. Refusing to engage opposing views signals weak argument and ego-driven writing.

questions.prompt: Briefly granting the opposing view's validity before refuting it is this rhetorical move.
questions.explanation: Concession briefly grants the opposing view's merit before reasserting the writer's position. 'While critics rightly note X, the evidence still favors Y.' W.9-10.1b explicitly requires fair counterclaim development. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays heavily reward strategic concession. Refusing concession looks defensive. Concession builds ethos by demonstrating fairness.

questions.prompt: Directly disproving an opposing view through evidence and reasoning is this argument move.
questions.explanation: Refutation disproves the opposing view through evidence, logical analysis, or fallacy exposure. W.9-10.1b covers counterclaim development; refutation is the final step after acknowledgment and concession. King's Birmingham letter refutes the clergy's charges. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward refutation. AP Lang argument essays rate refutation quality as part of overall sophistication.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'however,' 'on the other hand,' and 'critics argue' signal this kind of shift.
questions.explanation: Counterclaim transitions signal the shift from claim to opposing view, then back. 'However,' 'on the other hand,' 'critics argue,' 'while some maintain.' W.9-10.1c explicitly requires linking major sections to clarify claim-counterclaim relationships. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward clear transition use. AP Lang scoring favors signposted argument structure. Without explicit transitions, readers lose the argumentative thread.

questions.prompt: When a writer concedes one point then asserts an even stronger position, this rhetorical effect occurs.
questions.explanation: Strategic concession-then-counter strengthens ethos: the writer appears fair, then more authoritative. 'Some say technology isolates teens, and certainly excessive screen time does pose risks—but research shows social media also enables marginalized teens to find community impossible offline.' W.9-10.1b explicitly covers fair counterclaim handling. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward ethos-building moves.

questions.prompt: Reproducing an author's exact words inside quotation marks creates this evidence type.
questions.explanation: Direct quotation reproduces exact words inside quotation marks with attribution. W.9-10.1b requires supporting claims with relevant evidence. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays require direct quotation from numbered sources. AP Lang argument essays reward strategic quotation. Direct quotation matters when the author's exact phrasing is significant; paraphrase matters when the idea, not wording, is the point. Overquoting weakens argument; strategic quotation strengthens it.

questions.prompt: Restating an author's idea in your own words while preserving meaning is called this.
questions.explanation: Paraphrase restates an author's idea in your own words, sentence-by-sentence, with attribution. Distinct from summary (compression) and direct quotation (verbatim). W.9-10.8 explicitly covers paraphrasing source material. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept paraphrase with attribution. AP Lang argument essays favor strategic mixing of quotation and paraphrase. Plagiarism warning: paraphrasing without attribution still counts as plagiarism.

questions.prompt: Following a quotation with 1-2 sentences explaining its meaning and relevance creates this writing move.
questions.explanation: Commentary (analysis) follows evidence with the writer's interpretation of meaning and relevance. The PEEL paragraph (Point, Evidence, Explanation, Link) bakes commentary in. W.9-10.1b requires supplying evidence AND reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly require analysis, not just evidence dump. AP Lang argument essays score 'sophistication' partly through commentary depth. Strong commentary explains why the evidence matters, not just what it says.

questions.prompt: Arguing that one small step leads inevitably to disaster commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: Slippery slope fallacy assumes one action will inevitably lead to a chain of negative consequences without evidence for the chain. 'If we allow same-sex marriage, next people will marry their pets.' W.9-10.1b requires sound reasoning. RI.9-10.8 covers fallacy evaluation. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test recognition. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score fallacy detection. Distinct from legitimate prediction (which provides evidence for each step).

questions.prompt: Embedding a short phrase from a source inside your own sentence rather than as a block quote uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Quotation integration weaves short quotations seamlessly into the writer's own sentences. 'King's reference to 'the heat of injustice' makes systemic racism feel physically oppressive.' Distinct from block quotation (set off as separate paragraph) and dropped quotation (standing alone without integration). W.9-10.1d covers establishing and maintaining a formal style. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays reward smooth integration as a sophistication marker.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'furthermore' and 'moreover' signal this kind of relationship between ideas.
questions.explanation: Addition transitions ('furthermore,' 'moreover,' 'additionally,' 'in addition,' 'also,' 'similarly') signal that the next idea adds support or evidence. W.9-10.1c requires linking major sections with transitions. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score cohesion. AP Lang argument essays reward varied, purposeful transitions. Avoid overusing 'also' or starting every paragraph with 'another reason'—boring transitions signal weak craft.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'however' and 'on the other hand' signal this kind of relationship.
questions.explanation: Contrast transitions ('however,' 'on the other hand,' 'on the contrary,' 'nevertheless,' 'whereas,' 'although,' 'while') signal opposition or unexpected shift. Essential for counterargument structure. W.9-10.1c covers transitions clarifying claim-counterclaim relationships. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays heavily use contrast transitions. AP Lang argument essays demand strategic contrast handling.

questions.prompt: Briefly introducing a quotation with 'According to King...' uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Signal phrases introduce quotations with context: 'As King argues...' 'According to Source 2...' 'Douglass, writing from personal experience, observes...' W.9-10.1c covers cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly require source attribution; failure drops a score band. AP Lang argument essays favor varied signal phrases. Effective signal phrases include the writer's authority or perspective. Avoid repetitive 'X says' constructions.

questions.prompt: Repeating key terms across paragraphs to build connection is this cohesion technique.
questions.explanation: Lexical chains weave repeated key terms and their synonyms across paragraphs to build textual cohesion. An essay on Streetcar's symbolism might chain 'symbol,' 'symbolic,' 'represents,' 'embodies,' 'stands for.' W.9-10.1c covers cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score writing quality including cohesion. AP Lang argument essays reward sophisticated lexical handling. Halliday and Hasan's 1976 cohesion theory underlies this analysis.

questions.prompt: When each paragraph's first sentence references the previous paragraph's last, this technique occurs.
questions.explanation: Bridge sentences (linked transitions) create flow by referencing the previous paragraph at the start of a new one. 'Beyond Stanley's brute vitality, Williams also positions Mitch as a parallel kind of Southern decline.' W.9-10.1c covers paragraph-level cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward strong paragraph transitions. AP Lang argument essays score essays as wholes; paragraph linkage is a sophistication marker.

questions.prompt: Attacking the person rather than their argument commits this logical fallacy.
questions.explanation: Ad hominem (Latin: 'to the person') attacks speaker character instead of refuting argument. 'You can't trust King's views on segregation because he committed marital infidelities' is ad hominem. W.9-10.1b requires fair counterclaim handling. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays penalize this fallacy in student writing and may test recognition in sources. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score reasoning quality.

questions.prompt: Misrepresenting an opponent's argument to make it easier to refute commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: Straw man fallacy distorts the opposing argument into a weaker version. 'Critics want zero technology in schools'—when critics actually want limits. W.9-10.1b requires fair counterclaim handling. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test recognition. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays reward fair representation. Steel-manning (presenting the strongest version of opposition) is the opposite virtue.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'therefore' and 'consequently' signal this kind of relationship.
questions.explanation: Cause-effect transitions ('therefore,' 'thus,' 'consequently,' 'as a result,' 'hence,' 'so') signal logical or causal relationships. Critical in argument writing because they show how evidence supports claims. W.9-10.1c covers transitions. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward causal connectors. AP Lang argument essays score reasoning quality; explicit causal transitions show the warrant explicitly.

questions.prompt: Assuming Y must have caused X because Y happened first commits this Latin-named fallacy.
questions.explanation: Post hoc ergo propter hoc ('after this, therefore because of this') assumes sequence implies causation. 'Crime rose after the new mayor took office, so the mayor caused crime.' W.9-10.1b requires sound reasoning. RI.9-10.8 covers evaluating reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test recognition. AP Lang argument essays explicitly require sound causal reasoning. Correlation does not equal causation.

questions.prompt: Presenting only two options when more exist commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: False dilemma (false dichotomy) presents only two options when more exist. 'Either we ban guns or our children die.' RI.9-10.8 requires evaluating reasoning. Other common fallacies: ad hominem, straw man, slippery slope, hasty generalization, post hoc, appeal to authority. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often plant a fallacy in one source for students to detect. AP Lang argument essays rigorously test fallacy identification.

questions.prompt: Beginning a narrative in the middle of action, like The Odyssey's opening, uses this Latin-named technique.
questions.explanation: In medias res (Latin: 'in the middle of things') begins narrative in mid-action, then fills in background. The Iliad opens with Achilles's anger already raging. W.9-10.3a requires engaging openings. Regents Part 4 text-analysis response and AP Lang argument writing reward strong openings. The technique creates immediate stakes and pulls readers in. Modern fiction uses it heavily (Beloved opens 'with mid-action and traumatic memory').

questions.prompt: Opening with a startling fact or statistic to seize attention uses this hook strategy.
questions.explanation: Statistic hooks open with a startling number: 'Every twelve minutes, an American teen attempts suicide.' Effective in expository and argumentative writing when data is credible and relevant. W.9-10.2a covers engaging openings for informational/explanatory texts. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept this opening. AP Lang argument essays reward credible statistic openings but penalize fabricated or irrelevant numbers. Always cite the source.

questions.prompt: Opening with a brief, vivid story to illustrate a larger point uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Anecdote opens an essay with a brief, vivid story illustrating a larger point. Effective in expository, argumentative, and narrative writing. W.9-10.3a covers narrative openings. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes accept anecdotal openings. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays praise them when the anecdote sets up the broader argument. Beware: anecdotes must support, not substitute for, broader evidence.

questions.prompt: A narrative that hints at future events through suggestion uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Foreshadowing plants hints of future events to build suspense or thematic resonance. Julius Caesar's soothsayer warning 'Beware the ides of March' foreshadows assassination. W.9-10.3c covers narrative techniques. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from recognizing foreshadowing in source texts. AP Lit narrative writing rewards strategic foreshadowing; analysis essays prize identification of foreshadowing's thematic effect.

questions.prompt: Opening with a provocative question to engage readers' curiosity creates this hook type.
questions.explanation: Rhetorical questions don't expect literal answers; they engage readers in thinking. Frederick Douglass's 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852) begins with this question. W.9-10.3a covers engaging openings; W.9-10.2a covers expository openings. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes use rhetorical questions. AP Lang argument and narrative essays accept rhetorical questions when purposeful, not gimmicky.

questions.prompt: Sensory details engage these five channels of perception in narrative writing.
questions.explanation: Effective narrative engages sight, sound, smell, taste, touch.
