W.9-10.3d explicitly requires 'precise words and phrases, telling details, and sensory language to convey a vivid picture of experiences, events, setting, and/or characters.' Beginning writers over-rely on sight; smell and sound are underused and powerful. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards vivid prose. AP Lit narrative writing rewards sensory range as sophistication.

questions.prompt: Demonstrating a character is angry through clenched fists rather than stating 'she was angry' uses this principle.
questions.explanation: 'Show, don't tell' demands writers demonstrate emotion, character, and meaning through action, dialogue, and sensory detail rather than stating directly. Instead of 'Scout was scared,' write 'Scout's hand tightened around Atticus's coat sleeve.' W.9-10.3d explicitly requires sensory language to convey experience. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards showing. AP Lit narrative scenes reward showing as a sophistication marker.

questions.prompt: Description using unexpected comparison, like 'her laugh sounded like breaking glass,' creates this device.
questions.explanation: Imagery uses sensory language and figurative comparison to create vivid mental pictures or sensations. Strong imagery often pairs sense with simile or metaphor: 'her laugh sounded like breaking glass.' W.9-10.3d requires sensory language. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards imagery. AP Lit narrative and rhetorical analysis essays both score imagery use. Distinguished from mere description, imagery does more than catalog; it interprets.

questions.prompt: Opening with a striking image or sound that drops readers into a scene uses this hook.
questions.explanation: Sensory hooks open with immediate sensory immersion: the heat of a New Orleans summer evening, the salt-spray of an island shore. W.9-10.3a explicitly requires 'engaging' narrative openings with 'precise words and phrases, telling details, and sensory language.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards engagement. AP Lit narrative openings often reward sensory immediacy. Strong sensory openings model the descriptive technique the writer will use throughout.

questions.prompt: Deliberately slowing narrative time to emphasize a moment, like Lennie's death scene, uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Scene expansion stretches narrative time over a brief story moment to emphasize its weight. Steinbeck slows the final pages of Of Mice and Men around George shooting Lennie. W.9-10.3c covers pacing 'to develop experiences, events, and/or characters.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from pacing awareness. AP Lit narrative scenes prize controlled pacing as sophistication. Distinct from summary (which compresses) and ellipsis (which omits).

questions.prompt: The five-part pattern of exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution is this.
questions.explanation: Freytag's Pyramid (Gustav Freytag, 1863) maps five-part dramatic structure: exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution/denouement. It describes classical tragedy and most popular narrative. W.9-10.3a covers narrative organization. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards understanding of structure. AP Lit narrative writing and analysis require structural awareness. Modern narratives may invert or fragment the pyramid for effect.

questions.prompt: A narrative that returns to earlier events to fill in background uses this temporal device.
questions.explanation: Flashback (analepsis, in Genette's terms) interrupts present-tense narrative with scenes from earlier in time. The Joy Luck Club uses extensive flashback as mothers narrate pre-1949 China. W.9-10.3c covers narrative techniques including 'pacing.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from structural sophistication. AP Lit narrative analysis frequently asks about temporal manipulation. Distinct from frame narrative (which encloses one story in another).

questions.prompt: A narrative that resists chronological order to create thematic resonance uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Nonlinear narrative arranges events out of chronological order for thematic effect. The Joy Luck Club's structured cycling between China past and California present, Slaughterhouse-Five's time-jumping, Beloved's traumatic recurrences. W.9-10.3a covers organization; W.9-10.3c covers pacing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from recognizing structural sophistication. AP Lit narrative writing and analysis reward purposeful nonlinearity.

questions.prompt: Writing that explains how events produce specific outcomes uses this organizational pattern.
questions.explanation: Cause-effect organization explains how events produce outcomes. Can be single cause/multiple effects, multiple causes/single effect, or chains. W.9-10.2a covers organizing complex ideas. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing often analyzes cause-effect in source texts. AP Lang synthesis essays often hinge on causal reasoning. Avoid post hoc fallacies (mistaking sequence for causation). Strong causal writing acknowledges complexity.

questions.prompt: The rhythm and length pattern of sentences across a passage is called this.
questions.explanation: Sentence variety (syntactic diversity) mixes sentence lengths and structures: short punchy sentences for emphasis, longer flowing sentences for development, fragments for impact. Monotonous syntax bores readers. W.9-10.5 covers revision; W.9-10.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards varied syntax. AP Lang and AP Lit narrative writing and analysis prize syntactic range as sophistication.

questions.prompt: Writing that explains a process or defines a concept without arguing is called this.
questions.explanation: Expository (explanatory) writing informs without persuading: how-to guides, definitions, classifications, cause-effect explanations, compare-contrast essays. W.9-10.2 explicitly covers expository writing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing is partly expository (explaining the text). AP Lang's synthesis essays are largely expository. Distinct from argumentative (takes a position) and narrative (tells a story); expository writing prioritizes clarity.

questions.prompt: Comparing two texts by alternating subject A and subject B features uses this organization.
questions.explanation: Point-by-point compare-contrast alternates: feature 1 in A and B, feature 2 in A and B. Contrast with block organization (all of A, then all of B). W.9-10.2a explicitly covers organization for explanatory writing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing accepts either structure. AP Lang synthesis essays reward purposeful organizational choice. Point-by-point works for nuanced comparison; block works for brief contrasts.

questions.prompt: Using vivid verbs like 'stormed' instead of 'walked angrily' demonstrates this craft.
questions.explanation: Strong, specific verbs do more work than weak verbs propped up by adverbs. 'She walked angrily' is weaker than 'She stormed.' W.9-10.3d requires 'precise words and phrases.' W.9-10.2d covers precise language in informational writing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards strong verb choice. AP Lit narrative scenes prize specific verbs. Strunk and White's 'Elements of Style' (1959) made strong verbs canonical advice.

questions.prompt: Defining a term by category and distinguishing features is this expository move.
questions.explanation: Classification (formal) definition places a term in its larger category (genus) and identifies distinguishing features (species). 'A sonnet (genus: poem) is a 14-line poem with specific rhyme schemes (species).' W.9-10.2b covers definition use in expository techniques. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing sometimes requires definition. AP Lang synthesis and rhetorical analysis benefit from precise definition. Aristotle's logic underlies this technique.

questions.prompt: Concluding an essay by restating the topic and explaining its broader significance uses this move.
questions.explanation: The 'so what' conclusion explains why the topic matters beyond the essay itself. Weak conclusions paraphrase the thesis; strong ones extend its significance. W.9-10.2f explicitly requires a concluding statement that 'follows from and supports the information or explanation presented.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and Part 4 text-analysis writing both reward 'so what' conclusions. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays score sophistication via meaningful conclusions.

questions.prompt: The distinctive way a writer expresses ideas, recognizable across works, is called this.
questions.explanation: Voice is the writer's distinctive expression of personality through diction, syntax, and rhythm, recognizable across works. Toni Morrison's voice differs from Hemingway's. Beginning writers borrow voice; mature writers develop their own. W.9-10.3d covers narrative voice; W.9-10.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from voice analysis. AP Lang and AP Lit reward distinctive student voice.

questions.prompt: Long, complex sentences with multiple clauses building to a final main clause create this style.
questions.explanation: Periodic (hypotactic) style uses long, subordinated sentences with delayed main clauses, building momentum to a final point. Henry James, Faulkner, and Toni Morrison write in periodic styles. Contrast with paratactic (short, coordinate) style. W.9-10.4 covers style; W.9-10.5 covers revision. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from syntactic analysis. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward syntactic precision.

questions.prompt: Hemingway's brief, declarative sentences without subordination create this contrasting style.
questions.explanation: Paratactic style strings independent clauses without subordination, creating clipped, declarative effect. Hemingway's 'In the late summer of that year we lived in a house in a village...' is canonical. W.9-10.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards style awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize precise syntactic naming (paratactic vs hypotactic). Useful for contrast analysis.

questions.prompt: Replacing 'said' with 'whispered,' 'shouted,' or 'mumbled' affects this dimension of writing.
questions.explanation: Diction is word choice. Three levels: formal (academic), informal (conversational), colloquial (dialect, slang). Style-conscious writers vary diction strategically. W.9-10.5 covers revision for style; W.9-10.3d requires precise words. RL.9-10.4 covers how word choice shapes meaning. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both heavily score diction awareness.

questions.prompt: The voice and consciousness through which a narrative is told is this in fiction.
questions.explanation: Narrative perspective is the consciousness through which the story reaches the reader. Choices: first-person (I), second-person (you, rare), third-person (limited, omniscient, objective). W.9-10.3a requires establishing 'a point of view.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing requires perspective awareness in analysis. AP Lit narrative writing demands deliberate perspective choice; analysis essays often hinge on POV's effect on meaning.

questions.prompt: Two complete sentences joined with only a comma create this specific punctuation error.
questions.explanation: A comma splice joins two independent clauses with only a comma. 'Atticus defended Tom Robinson, he was the only lawyer who would.' Fixes: add a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS), use a semicolon, or split into two sentences. L.9-10.2 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes comma splices when they impede clarity. AP Lang allows occasional intentional splices for stylistic effect; unintentional ones lower writing quality.

questions.prompt: The seven conjunctions that join independent clauses spell this acronym.
questions.explanation: FANBOYS: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So—the seven coordinating conjunctions that can join independent clauses (preceded by a comma). L.9-10.2 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests grammar. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward clear sentence-level command. Subordinating conjunctions (because, although, since, while, etc.) join dependent clauses to independent ones. Knowing the difference enables varied syntax.

questions.prompt: Commas around 'who lived in Maycomb' in 'Atticus, who lived in Maycomb, was a lawyer' mark this clause type.
questions.explanation: Nonrestrictive (non-essential) clauses add information that could be removed without changing the sentence's core meaning. They take commas. Compare restrictive: 'The lawyer who defended Tom Robinson was Atticus' (no commas; specifies which lawyer). L.9-10.2a covers semicolons; L.9-10.2b covers commas. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests recognition of restrictive vs nonrestrictive. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise punctuation.

questions.prompt: Beginning a sentence with 'After the war ended,' uses this introductory element requiring a comma.
questions.explanation: Introductory adverbial phrases (prepositional, participial, infinitive) preceding the main clause take a comma. 'After the war ended, soldiers returned home.' 'Running through the woods, Rainsford spotted Zaroff.' L.9-10.2 explicitly covers punctuation conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both reward correct introductory comma usage. Failure to place introductory commas signals basic error and weakens credibility.

questions.prompt: The comma before 'and' in 'red, white, and blue' is called this disputed punctuation mark.
questions.explanation: The Oxford (serial) comma precedes the final conjunction in a series. Strong arguments for use: prevents ambiguity ('I dedicate this book to my parents, Ayn Rand and God' vs '...my parents, Ayn Rand, and God'). AP, MLA, and Chicago vary in requirement. L.9-10.2 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing follows standard conventions; AP Lang penalizes mechanical errors only when they impede understanding. Most teachers favor Oxford for clarity.

questions.prompt: A modifier without a clear subject in the sentence is called this.
questions.explanation: A dangling modifier has no clear noun to modify, creating awkward or unintentionally funny sentences. 'Running through the woods, the tree branches scratched her' implies branches were running. Fix: 'Running through the woods, she felt branches scratch her.' L.9-10.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests dangling modifier recognition. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays favor clear modifier placement. Often occurs with participial-phrase openings.

questions.prompt: A modifier placed too far from the word it describes creates this kind of error.
questions.explanation: Misplaced modifiers sit too far from the words they modify, creating ambiguity. 'She almost drove for six hours' (=almost six hours, or almost drove?). Fix: 'She drove for almost six hours.' L.9-10.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests modifier placement. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays score writing clarity. Common with 'only,' 'just,' 'almost,' 'nearly'—place these directly before the word modified.

questions.prompt: An adjective like 'green' describes a noun; this part of speech modifies verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs.
questions.explanation: Adverbs modify verbs (ran quickly), adjectives (very green), and other adverbs (extremely well). Most end in -ly but not all (fast, hard, well, soon). L.9-10.1 covers conventions; L.9-10.1a explicitly addresses 'various types of phrases and clauses' usage. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests grammatical accuracy. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays sometimes hinge on adverb effects (intensifiers, time/place markers). 'She felt bad' (adjective) vs 'She felt badly' (adverb, impaired touch).

questions.prompt: A phrase like 'sitting on the porch' functioning as an adjective is called this.
questions.explanation: Participial phrases use a present (-ing) or past (-ed) participle plus modifiers to function as an adjective. 'Sitting on the porch, the woman watched her children.' L.9-10.1a covers 'various types of phrases.' L.9-10.1b covers 'parallel structure.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests sentence-level grammar. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize varied phrasal openings. Participial phrases create syntactic variety and embed information efficiently.

questions.prompt: Placing equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms creates this kind of structure.
questions.explanation: Parallel structure places equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms. Hamlet's 'To be, or not to be' parallels infinitive verbs. King's 'I have a dream that... I have a dream that...' is parallel. L.9-10.1b explicitly requires 'parallel structure.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes faulty parallelism. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward parallel structure identification. Faulty: 'I like swimming, running, and to bike.' Fix: 'swimming, running, and biking.'.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'bene' meaning 'good' appears in 'benevolent,' 'benefit,' and this term for one who does good.
questions.explanation: The Latin root 'bene' (good) generates 'benevolent' (wishing well), 'benefit' (advantage), 'benefactor' (one who does good), 'benediction' (good speech, blessing). Common Core L.9-10.4b explicitly requires identifying Latin and Greek roots. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice and Part 3 source-based argument essays test vocabulary built on roots. AP Lang and AP Lit reward sophisticated vocabulary; roots enable inference of unfamiliar words. Contrast 'mal' (bad): malevolent, malefactor.

questions.prompt: The Greek root 'graph' meaning 'write' appears in 'autograph,' 'biography,' and this picture-writing term.
questions.explanation: Greek root 'graph' (write) generates 'autograph' (self-writing), 'biography' (life-writing), 'photograph' (light-writing), 'cartography' (map-writing), 'calligraphy' (beautiful writing). L.9-10.4b requires root and affix analysis. Regents Part 2 vocabulary questions reward root knowledge. AP Lang and AP Lit assume students can decode unfamiliar words via roots. Greek roots dominate scientific vocabulary; Latin roots dominate legal and academic vocabulary.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'spec' or 'spect' meaning 'look' appears in 'inspect' and this audience-related word.
questions.explanation: Latin root 'spec/spect' (look, see) generates 'inspect' (look into), 'spectator' (one who looks), 'spectacular' (worth looking at), 'speculate' (theorize), 'perspective' (look through), 'suspect' (doubt). L.9-10.4b requires roots and affixes. Regents Part 2 vocabulary tests root recognition. AP Lang and AP Lit assume root literacy. Latin morphology: prefix + root + suffix yields meaning through composition. Family also includes 'specimen,' 'specter,' 'aspect.'.

questions.prompt: The Greek root 'demos' meaning 'people' appears in 'democracy' and this term for a populist leader.
questions.explanation: Greek root 'demos' (people) generates 'democracy' (rule by the people), 'demographer' (people-writer, population scientist), 'demagogue' (people-leader, populist orator), 'endemic' (in the people, native), 'epidemic' (upon the people), 'pandemic' (all the people). L.9-10.4b covers roots. Regents Part 2 vocabulary and Part 3 source-based argument both reward root knowledge. AP Lang civics texts often use these roots. Plato's 'Republic' critiques 'demos' rule.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'dict' meaning 'say' appears in 'predict,' 'verdict,' and this 'ban' term.
questions.explanation: Latin root 'dict' (say, speak) generates 'dictate' (speak forth), 'predict' (speak before), 'contradict' (speak against), 'verdict' (truly spoken, judgment), 'edict' (proclamation), 'diction' (manner of speaking), 'interdict' (speak between, forbid). L.9-10.4b explicitly covers Latin and Greek roots. Regents Part 2 vocabulary tests these. AP Lang argument essays rely on precise diction; understanding 'dict' family deepens word-precision skill.

questions.prompt: Calling a king 'the crown' uses this device where a part stands for the whole.
questions.explanation: Synecdoche substitutes a part for the whole (or vice versa). 'All hands on deck' uses 'hands' for sailors. L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test synecdoche. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward identification. Often confused with metonymy (related but separate). Modern: 'the Pentagon decided' (the building for military leadership).

questions.prompt: Calling the U.S. president 'the White House' uses this related substitution device.
questions.explanation: Metonymy substitutes a closely associated thing: 'the White House' for the U.S. president, 'Wall Street' for the financial industry, 'the pen is mightier than the sword.' L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. Distinguished from synecdoche (which uses part-whole specifically) by being broader. Regents Part 2 vocabulary may test metonymy. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward precise device-naming.

questions.prompt: Combining contradictory terms like 'jumbo shrimp' or 'deafening silence' creates this device.
questions.explanation: Oxymoron pairs contradictory terms for striking effect: 'jumbo shrimp,' 'bittersweet,' 'living dead,' 'deafening silence.' From Greek 'oxys' (sharp) + 'moros' (dull). Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet builds an oxymoron catalogue at 1.1.169-178. L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests oxymoron recognition. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward precise identification. Often confused with paradox (which is a longer statement).

questions.prompt: A statement that seems contradictory but reveals truth, like 'less is more,' is this device.
questions.explanation: A paradox is a statement that appears self-contradictory but reveals deeper truth. 'Less is more' (more meaning by saying less). 'I must be cruel only to be kind' (Hamlet). Distinguished from oxymoron (a phrase) and contradiction (no truth). L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes hinge on paradoxical claims. AP Lang and AP Lit reward paradox recognition.

questions.prompt: Understating by negating the opposite, like 'not bad' for 'good,' uses this device.
questions.explanation: Litotes (LIE-toh-teez) understates by negating the opposite: 'not bad' for 'good,' 'no small problem' for 'a big problem.' L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. MLK's 'I am not unmindful that some of you have come here out of great trials and tribulations' uses litotes. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice rarely tests litotes; AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize identification. Anglo-Saxon literature (Beowulf) heavily uses litotes.

questions.prompt: Determining a word's meaning from surrounding text uses this strategy.
questions.explanation: Context clues are surrounding text that helps infer meaning: definition, example, contrast, restatement clues. L.9-10.4a explicitly requires 'use context (e.g., the overall meaning of a sentence, paragraph, or text; a word's position or function in a sentence) as a clue to the meaning of a word or phrase.' Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests vocabulary in context frequently. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays assume context-clue mastery.

questions.prompt: A word's literal dictionary definition is called this.
questions.explanation: Denotation is a word's literal, dictionary definition. Contrasts with connotation (associations). 'House' and 'home' have similar denotations but different connotations. L.9-10.4 covers vocabulary; L.9-10.5b explicitly distinguishes 'connotations (associations) of words with similar denotations (definitions).' Regents Part 2 multiple-choice often tests denotation/connotation distinction. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays must distinguish these for precise word-choice analysis.

questions.prompt: The emotional and cultural associations a word carries beyond its literal meaning are this.
questions.explanation: Connotation is the cluster of associations, emotions, and cultural baggage a word carries. 'Childish' and 'childlike' share denotation (relating to children) but opposite connotations. L.9-10.5b explicitly covers connotation/denotation. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests connotative awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays must analyze connotation as part of diction. Mastery requires extensive reading; connotations shift across cultures and eras.

questions.prompt: Words sharing essential meaning, like 'big' and 'enormous,' are these.
questions.explanation: Synonyms share core meaning but typically differ in connotation, formality, or precision. 'Big,' 'large,' 'enormous,' 'colossal' progress in intensity. L.9-10.6 covers vocabulary acquisition. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests synonym selection in context. AP Lang and AP Lit reward precise word selection; true synonyms are rare, and choosing among synonyms is the craft of writing. Roget's Thesaurus warns against treating synonyms as interchangeable.

questions.prompt: Words from one language that have entered another, like 'kindergarten' or 'safari,' are these.
questions.explanation: Loanwords are borrowings from one language into another. English has absorbed 'kindergarten' (German), 'safari' (Swahili), 'sushi' (Japanese), 'siesta' (Spanish). L.9-10.4 covers vocabulary; L.9-10.6 covers acquiring and using accurate words. English's loanword saturation (over half its vocabulary is borrowed, mostly French/Latin/Greek) makes etymology critical. Regents Part 2 vocabulary may test borrowed-word meaning.

questions.prompt: Shakespeare's tragedy about the assassination and revenge for a Roman general.
questions.explanation: Shakespeare's Julius Caesar (1599) dramatizes the conspiracy of Brutus and Cassius, the assassination of Caesar, and the civil war that follows. RL.9-10.2 covers theme; RL.9-10.5 covers structure. Antony's funeral oration in 3.2 is canonical rhetoric. Common Core lists Shakespeare among the foundational texts for Grades 9-10. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on conspiracy, rhetoric, and power use this play. AP Lit treats it as a teaching text for tragedy.

questions.prompt: Restating an author's idea in your own words while preserving meaning is this.
questions.explanation: Paraphrase restates an author's idea in your own words with attribution. Distinct from summary (compression) and quotation (verbatim). W.9-10.8 covers paraphrasing source material. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept paraphrase with attribution. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays reward strategic mixing of quotation and paraphrase. Plagiarism warning: paraphrasing without attribution counts as plagiarism.

questions.prompt: Irony when the audience knows something a character does not is this kind.
questions.explanation: Dramatic irony occurs when the audience knows something a character does not, creating tension between knowledge levels. RL.9-10.5 covers how authors structure texts for effect. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests irony types. AP Lit narrative analysis demands precise irony naming: verbal (saying opposite), situational (event-outcome mismatch), dramatic (audience-character gap).

questions.prompt: An extended narrative where characters represent abstract ideas, like Lord of the Flies' island.
questions.explanation: Allegory sustains a narrative in which characters, events, and settings represent abstract ideas. Lord of the Flies allegorizes civilization's fragility through stranded boys; Animal Farm allegorizes the Russian Revolution. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats allegory as a structural and thematic device. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on power and human nature use these allegorical texts.

questions.prompt: Chinua Achebe's novel of Okonkwo's pre-colonial Igbo world meeting British missionaries.
questions.explanation: Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart (1958) follows Okonkwo's Igbo clan in 1890s Nigeria as British colonizers and missionaries arrive. The title comes from Yeats's 'The Second Coming.' RL.9-10.2 covers theme; RL.9-10.6 covers point of view. Common Core lists Things Fall Apart as exemplar for Grade 10. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on tradition, change, and colonialism use this novel. AP Lit treats Achebe as foundational postcolonial author.

questions.prompt: A direct comparison without 'like' or 'as,' like 'Juliet is the sun.'.
questions.explanation: A metaphor equates two unlike things to suggest shared qualities, without 'like' or 'as.' RL.9-10.4 explicitly covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests metaphor identification; Part 3 source-based arguments often analyze metaphor's role. AP Lang and AP Lit demand precise metaphor-effect analysis. Extended metaphors (conceits) develop across passages.

questions.prompt: Tennessee Williams's drama featuring Stanley Kowalski and Blanche DuBois in New Orleans.
questions.explanation: Tennessee Williams's A Streetcar Named Desire (1947) dramatizes Blanche DuBois's collision with Stanley Kowalski's working-class New Orleans world. The 1948 Pulitzer Prize winner. RL.9-10.3 covers character; RL.9-10.5 covers structure. AP Lit treats Streetcar as canonical American drama. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on illusion, class, or gender use this play. Williams's stage directions and symbolic naming reward close reading.

questions.prompt: Williams's 'streetcar named Desire' transferring to 'Cemeteries' uses this device.
questions.explanation: Williams takes literal New Orleans streetcar names and uses them symbolically to map Blanche's psychological journey: Desire (her past disgrace) to Cemeteries (her dead family) to Elysian Fields (her arrival). RL.9-10.4 covers figurative language. AP Lit prizes symbolic detail analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on illusion and reality use Streetcar. Williams's symbol-saturated drama rewards close reading.

questions.prompt: Elie Wiesel's memoir of his deportation and Auschwitz survival.
questions.explanation: Elie Wiesel's Night (1958/1960) recounts his deportation from Sighet, Romania to Auschwitz-Birkenau at age 15, his survival, and his father's death. Wiesel won the 1986 Nobel Peace Prize. RI.9-10.6 covers memoir; RL.9-10.2 covers theme. Common Core lists Night for Grades 9-10. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on Holocaust, faith, and witness use this memoir. AP Lang treats it as canonical creative nonfiction.

questions.prompt: A character whose contrast highlights another character's traits is this.
questions.explanation: A foil is a character whose traits contrast with another's to illuminate that character. Brutus/Cassius (idealism vs envy), Stanley/Blanche (vitality vs refinement), Ralph/Jack (democracy vs authoritarianism). RL.9-10.3 covers character analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often analyze foils. AP Lit analysis essays prize precise foil identification and effect explanation.

questions.prompt: The author's specific, arguable position in an argumentative essay is this.
questions.explanation: A thesis is the writer's specific, arguable, defensible position. W.9-10.1a requires precise claims. Strong theses are debatable, specific, and forecast organization. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score thesis quality heavily. AP Lang argument essays require thesis identification and defense. The thesis controls organization: every paragraph supports it. Beginning writers confuse thesis with topic.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's three rhetorical appeals are ethos, pathos, and this.
questions.explanation: Aristotle's three artistic proofs are ethos (speaker's credibility), pathos (emotional appeal), and logos (logical appeal through evidence and reasoning). RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both require Aristotelian appeal identification. Strong rhetoric balances all three; over-reliance on any one weakens persuasion.

questions.prompt: Repeating a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses, like MLK's 'I have a dream,' creates this.
questions.explanation: Anaphora repeats a word or phrase at the start of successive clauses. King's eight 'I have a dream' repetitions, Lincoln's 'we cannot dedicate...' RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric; L.9-10.5 covers figurative language. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward anaphora identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise device-naming. Distinguish anaphora from epistrophe (ending repetition).

questions.prompt: Logical errors like ad hominem and straw man are collectively called these.
questions.explanation: Logical fallacies are reasoning errors that may seem persuasive but fail logically. Common: ad hominem, straw man, post hoc, hasty generalization, false dilemma, slippery slope. RI.9-10.8 explicitly requires evaluating reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward fallacy identification.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's framework of writer, audience, and subject forms this rhetorical figure.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical triangle (Aristotle) connects writer/speaker (ethos), audience (pathos), and subject (logos). Effective rhetoric balances all three angles. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. AP Lang's rhetorical analysis essay is built on this framework. Mapping the triangle answers: who is speaking, to whom, about what, and how the choices serve all three points.

questions.prompt: Demonstrating a character is angry through action rather than telling readers uses this principle.
questions.explanation: 'Show, don't tell' demands writers demonstrate emotion, character, and meaning through action, dialogue, and sensory detail rather than stating it directly. W.9-10.3d requires sensory language to convey experience. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards showing. AP Lit narrative writing scores showing heavily. Foundational rule of fiction craft, attributed variously to Henry James and Anton Chekhov.

questions.prompt: Placing equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms creates this kind of structure.
questions.explanation: Parallel structure places equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms. Caesar's 'Veni, vidi, vici' is canonical. L.9-10.1b explicitly requires parallel structure. Faulty: 'I like swimming, running, and to bike.' Fix: 'swimming, running, and biking.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests parallelism. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward parallelism identification in source texts.

questions.prompt: The opposing view a writer must acknowledge in argument writing is this.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is the opposing position a writer addresses to strengthen the main claim. W.9-10.1b explicitly requires developing 'claim(s) and counterclaims fairly.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays evaluate counterclaim acknowledgment. AP Lang argument essays heavily reward genuine counterclaim engagement. Refusing counterclaims signals weak argument.

questions.prompt: The deliberate arrangement of words for effect, including sentence length and structure, is this.
questions.explanation: Syntax is the arrangement of words in sentences, including order, length, and clause patterns. Periodic syntax (long, delayed main clause) builds suspense; paratactic syntax (short, declarative) creates impact. L.9-10.1 covers conventions; L.9-10.3 covers language knowledge. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards syntactic awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise syntax-effect analysis.

questions.prompt: The speed and rhythm of narrative time, slowed for emphasis or quickened for summary, is this.
questions.explanation: Pacing is the speed of narrative time relative to story time. Writers slow pace through scene, dialogue, and description; quicken through summary and ellipsis. W.9-10.3c explicitly requires pacing as a narrative technique. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards pacing awareness. AP Lit narrative writing prizes deliberate pacing; analysis essays often hinge on how pacing develops character or theme.

questions.prompt: Two complete sentences joined with only a comma create this specific error.
questions.explanation: A comma splice joins two independent clauses with only a comma. Fixes: add a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS), use a semicolon, or split. L.9-10.2 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes comma splices when they impede clarity. AP Lang scoring affects rhetorical effectiveness when errors mount. Distinct from run-on sentences (which lack any punctuation between clauses).

questions.prompt: The emotional and cultural associations of a word, beyond its literal meaning, are this.
questions.explanation: Connotation is the cluster of associations, emotions, and cultural baggage a word carries. 'Childish' and 'childlike' share denotation but opposite connotations. L.9-10.5b explicitly covers connotation/denotation. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests connotative awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand connotation analysis as part of diction. Mastery requires extensive reading.

questions.prompt: J.D. Salinger's novel narrated by 16-year-old Holden Caulfield in New York.
questions.explanation: J.D. Salinger's The Catcher in the Rye (1951) is narrated by 16-year-old Holden Caulfield as he wanders New York after expulsion from Pencey Prep. RL.9-10.3 covers character; RL.9-10.6 covers point of view (Holden as unreliable narrator). Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on adolescent identity, grief, and authenticity use this novel. AP Lit treats it as canonical first-person narration. The novel was the most-banned book in U.S. schools, 1973-78.

questions.prompt: A modifier without a clear subject, like 'Running through the woods, the branches scratched her,' is this.
questions.explanation: A dangling modifier has no clear noun to modify, creating awkward sentences. 'Running through the woods, the tree branches scratched her' implies branches were running. Fix: 'Running through the woods, she felt branches scratch her.' L.9-10.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests recognition. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays favor clear modifier placement.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'dict' meaning 'say' appears in 'predict' and this term for an authoritative pronouncement.
questions.explanation: Latin root 'dict' (say, speak) generates 'predict' (speak before), 'verdict' (truly spoken, judgment), 'edict' (proclamation), 'interdict' (forbid), 'diction' (manner of speaking). L.9-10.4b explicitly covers Latin and Greek roots. Regents Part 2 vocabulary tests these. AP Lang argument essays rely on precise diction; understanding the 'dict' family deepens word-precision skill.
